critic_raw:
This case has the same fundamental RL evaluation error as case 788 but in a retail context. Deep reinforcement learning learns policies through sequential state-action-reward interactions. Splitting historical inventory data into train/val/test sets and evaluating a DRL model on the held-out "test set" is not a valid RL evaluation. The DRL agent learns from trajectories generated by the historical policy (the rule-based system's decisions), but historical trajectories reflect what happened under the rule-based system's actions — not what would have happened under the DRL agent's actions. Measuring the DRL agent's performance on held-out historical data is measuring how well it approximates the rule-based policy, not how well it optimizes the inventory objective.

The design explicitly acknowledges that "temporal autocorrelation is weak, requiring further validation" — this is an admission that the core justification for the stratified random split is unverified. Supply chain inventory data is not temporally stationary: seasonal demand cycles, promotional events, and supply disruptions create strong temporal dependencies. Randomly distributing stockout events across splits will put summer promotional stockout events in both training and test, allowing the model to exploit known seasonal patterns rather than discovering them from training data alone.

The DRL framework is compared against a "manually defined heuristics" baseline that is "appropriately tuned to manage similar inventory costs." It is unclear what "appropriately tuned" means for a heuristic system without a principled optimization criterion. If the heuristic is tuned on the training set using grid search over reorder points and safety stock levels, it is a strong baseline; if it is a fixed industry-standard heuristic, the comparison is not between an optimized DRL agent and an optimized heuristic.

Stockout rate alone as the primary metric ignores the inventory-level tradeoff. DRL agents, absent an explicit overstock penalty in the reward function, will systematically overstock to minimize the stockout penalty. The design does not specify the reward function, which is the most critical design choice for DRL. A poorly designed reward function (stockout penalty without overstock cost) produces a policy that trivially maximizes the evaluation metric without solving the real problem.

The reward function specification is completely absent from the methodology description. For a DRL experiment, the reward function is the primary design choice — it defines what the agent learns to optimize. Omitting it makes the experimental design uninterpretable.
defender_raw:
The deep reinforcement learning inventory management design has a principled motivation but shares the same fundamental evaluation design problem as eval_scenario_788, which the defense must acknowledge.

DRL is more sophisticated than Q-learning for complex retail inventory management. The design's choice of deep RL is motivated by the high-dimensional state space (multiple SKUs, multiple outlets, lead time distributions, promotional calendars) that tabular Q-learning cannot represent. Neural network function approximation for policy and value estimation is the appropriate architectural response to this dimensionality.

The feature set — daily sales, stock metrics, lead times, promotions — is well-specified. The explicit inclusion of promotional event records is critical because promotional demand spikes are the primary cause of short-term stockouts, and a model without promotional awareness will fail predictably in exactly the scenarios that matter most to retailers.

Stockout rate as the primary metric with inventory cost and average inventory levels as secondaries is the correct performance framework. Stockout reduction is the business objective, but cost and inventory level secondaries prevent the degenerate solution of simply over-ordering everything (which eliminates stockouts at the expense of carrying cost and spoilage).

The rule-based baseline with manually defined heuristics 'appropriately tuned to manage similar inventory costs' is correctly framed. This prevents the trivial win of a DRL model that achieves better stockout rates only by carrying excess inventory relative to the heuristic.

The stratified random split critique from eval_scenario_788 applies here too and arguably more severely: DRL policies are learned over multi-step trajectories where each action depends on the cumulative prior state. Randomly splitting daily records breaks these trajectories. The design explicitly acknowledges 'temporal autocorrelation is weak, requiring further validation,' which demonstrates self-awareness of the limitation. This acknowledgment is methodologically honest.

External demand features as confound controls correctly address business cycle effects on demand variation.
all_issues_raised: Applying supervised train/val/test split to DRL is a category error: DRL policies must be evaluated through their own action sequences, not on historical trajectories generated by a different policy; without off-policy correction, the evaluation measures policy imitation, not policy quality.
The temporal autocorrelation weakness assumption for the stratified random split is explicitly unverified in the design; seasonal supply chain dynamics make random split inappropriate.
The reward function for the DRL agent is entirely unspecified — this is the primary design choice for RL and its omission makes the experimental design uninterpretable.

An absence of overstock penalty in the reward function (which is unspecified) will cause the DRL agent to eliminate stockouts by overstocking, trivially maximizing the primary metric while failing the real business objective.
Heuristic baseline tuning is underspecified — 'appropriately tuned' is not a verifiable standard, and the level of optimization effort for the baseline determines whether the comparison is fair.
all_issues_adjudicated: Applying supervised train/val/test split to DRL is a category error: DRL policies must be evaluated through their own action sequences, not on historical trajectories generated by a different policy; without off-policy correction, the evaluation measures policy imitation, not policy quality.
The reward function for the DRL agent is entirely unspecified — this is the primary design choice for RL and its omission makes the experimental design uninterpretable.

An absence of overstock penalty in the reward function (which is unspecified) will cause the DRL agent to eliminate stockouts by overstocking, trivially maximizing the primary metric while failing the real business objective.
